pages.title: Overview
pages.body:
Five rotating primitives demonstrate Artlab's geometry factories and MeshStandardMaterial. Each shape is defined once in the SHAPES array, then built in a loop inside setup().

Navigate through the pages below to walk through each section of this file.

pages.title: DOM Label Helper
pages.body:
makeLabelEl() creates a fixed-position <div> that floats over the canvas. It returns the element without appending it — the caller decides where to place it.

This pattern is reusable: keep DOM creation in a helper function and lifecycle management (append / remove) in setup() and teardown().

pages.title: Camera & Lights
pages.body:
setup() positions the camera and adds three lights:

· AmbientLight — raises the shadow floor so dark sides aren't pure black
· DirectionalLight — the primary "sun" from upper-right
· PointLight — a blue rim from behind-left to add depth

Three-light rigs like this approximate classic studio photography.

pages.title: Creating Meshes
pages.body:
For each entry in SHAPES, a geometry is created from the factory function stored in def.geo, then combined with a MeshStandardMaterial into a Mesh.

Note: emissive is set to a dim version of the base color. This gives each mesh a subtle self-glow that prevents it from going completely dark on the unlit side — especially useful under directional lighting.

pages.title: Animation in update()
pages.body:
update() is called every frame. Each mesh spins on Y using dt (delta time in seconds), so motion is framerate-independent. X rotation and Y position use Math.sin(elapsed + offset) to phase-shift the meshes apart.

The label positions are recomputed each frame by projecting the mesh's 3D position into screen space using Three.js's project() method.

pages.title: Teardown
pages.body: teardown() removes only the DOM elements this example created. Three.js meshes added via ctx.add() are cleaned up automatically by the Artlab runtime when the example unloads.
